title: Integrate e-waste into digital growth planning
body: Digital economy policy, broadband plans, and inclusion strategies should all carry e-waste impact assessments rather than treating hardware waste as an afterthought.
action: Require every major digital growth framework to account for material waste costs.

title: Make EPR enforceable at scale
body: Mandatory registration, auditable recycling targets, and penalties for non-compliance are needed if EPR is to move beyond paper architecture.
action: Back EPRON and NESREA with enforcement capacity, targets, and public compliance pressure.

title: Use the 2025 Basel amendments aggressively
body: Nigeria now has stronger legal tools for controlling e-waste shipments, but those tools mean little without inspection capacity and customs training.
action: Invest in port-level inspection systems and customs readiness for prior informed consent enforcement.

title: Press for transboundary EPR fee transfer
body: Producer responsibility fees collected in exporting countries should follow products when they move into Nigerian markets as used electronics.
action: Treat lifecycle cost transfer as a core fairness demand in international e-waste diplomacy.

title: Formalise the informal sector around rights, not paperwork
body: Registration alone will not solve toxic exposure, weak bargaining power, or income insecurity in informal recycling.
action: Tie formalisation to labour protections, health coverage, and livable income safeguards.

title: Institutionalise health impact monitoring
body: Mortality and groundwater findings require a standing policy response rather than one-off research attention.
action: Monitor major sites, remediate contaminated water, and restrict residential expansion around active hotspots.
